number-fields-integers.title: Number fields and rings of integers

questions.q: For squarefree $d$, the ring of integers of $K=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{d})$ is:
questions.explain: The element $\tfrac{1+\sqrt{d}}{2}$ is an algebraic integer precisely when $d\equiv 1\pmod 4$ (its minimal polynomial $x^2-x+\tfrac{1-d}{4}$ then has integer coefficients).

questions.q: In the Gaussian integers $\mathbb{Z}[i]$, compute the product $(2+i)(1-i)$ as $a+bi$.
Which statement characterizes the rational primes that ramify in a number field $K/\mathbb{Q}$?
choices: $p$ ramifies iff $p$ is inert in some quadratic subfield
every prime ramifies in every number field
questions.explain: The discriminant exactly records ramification: $p$ ramifies in $\mathcal{O}_K$ iff $p\mid\Delta_K$. In particular only finitely many primes ramify.

questions.q: For $K=\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_5)$, the cyclotomic discriminant is $\Delta_K=(-1)^{(5-1)/2}\cdot 5^{5-2}$. Compute $|\Delta_K|$.
questions.explain: $|\Delta_{\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_p)}|=p^{p-2}$ for an odd prime $p$; for $p=5$, that is $5^3=125$. (Only the prime $5$ ramifies, as expected.)

questions.q: In a number field $K$ of degree $n$, which identity relates the ramification indices $e_i$ and residue degrees $f_i$ to $n$?
questions.explain: The fundamental equality $\sum_{i=1}^g e_i f_i = n$ comes from dimension-counting $\mathcal{O}_K/p\mathcal{O}_K$ as an $\mathbb{F}_p$-vector space.

questions.q: For odd prime $p$ with $p\nmid d$ in $K=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{d})$, the prime $p$ is inert exactly when:
questions.explain: If $(d/p)=-1$ then $x^2-d$ is irreducible mod $p$, so $p\mathcal{O}_K$ stays prime: $e=1$, $f=2$, $g=1$.

questions.q: In $\mathcal{O}_K=\mathbb{Z}[\sqrt{-5}]$ the prime $p=2$ ramifies as $(2)=\mathfrak{p}^2$. What is the ramification index $e$?
questions.explain: When $p\mid\Delta_K$ (here $\Delta_K=-20$), the prime ramifies; in the quadratic case the exponent on the unique prime above is $e=2$.

minkowski-bound.title: Minkowski bound and geometry of numbers

questions.q: Minkowski's convex body theorem says a centrally symmetric convex body $S\subset\mathbb{R}^n$ contains a nonzero lattice point of $\Lambda$ whenever:
questions.explain: The sharp threshold is $2^n\operatorname{covol}(\Lambda)$: translating $\tfrac12 S$ by $\Lambda$ forces two translates to overlap, producing a nonzero lattice point in $S$.

questions.q: Compute the Minkowski bound $M_K=(4/\pi)^{r_2}(n!/n^n)\sqrt{|\Delta_K|}$ for $K=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{-5})$ (so $n=2$, $r_2=1$, $|\Delta_K|=20$). Answer to 4 decimal places.
questions.explain: $M_K=(4/\pi)^1\cdot(2!/2^2)\cdot\sqrt{20}=(4/\pi)\cdot(1/2)\cdot 2\sqrt{5}=\tfrac{4\sqrt{5}}{\pi}\approx 2.8471$. Enumerating primes of norm $\le 2$ reduces the class group to $\langle[\mathfrak{p}_2]\rangle$, giving $h=2$.

questions.q: Why does the Minkowski bound imply the class group $\operatorname{Cl}(\mathcal{O}_K)$ is finite?
choices: because it shows every ideal is principal
because every ideal class has an integral representative of norm $\le M_K$, and there are only finitely many ideals of bounded norm
because Minkowski's theorem proves $\mathcal{O}_K$ is a PID
questions.explain: Every class contains a representative $I$ with $N(I)\le M_K$, and for any bound $B$ only finitely many integral ideals satisfy $N(I)\le B$ (each such ideal lies above a rational prime $p\le B$).

questions.q: By Dirichlet's unit theorem, for a number field $K$ of signature $(r_1,r_2)$ the unit group satisfies:
questions.explain: Dirichlet: $\mathcal{O}_K^\times\cong\mu_K\times\mathbb{Z}^{r_1+r_2-1}$, where $\mu_K$ is the finite group of roots of unity in $K$.

questions.q: What is the unit rank of $K=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{7})$?
questions.explain: Real quadratic fields have signature $(r_1,r_2)=(2,0)$, so the unit rank is $r_1+r_2-1=2+0-1=1$.

questions.q: For $K=\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{-5})$, the ideal class group has order $h_K=2$. Since the class group is trivial iff $\mathcal{O}_K$ is a PID, enter $0$ if $\mathbb{Z}[\sqrt{-5}]$ is a PID and $1$ if it is not.
questions.explain: With $h_K=2$, $\mathbb{Z}[\sqrt{-5}]$ is not a PID — the classical failure of unique factorization, $6=2\cdot 3=(1+\sqrt{-5})(1-\sqrt{-5})$.
